food_name: Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal (Sambal-Stuffed Hardtail Mackerel)
food_subgroup: Deep-fried stuffed fish — Malay traditional preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sambal-stuffed deep-fried hardtail mackerel specifically; mapped to closest parent mackerel food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for this traditional Malay stuffed-and-fried fish preparation; A00GJ (cooked fish) used as the nearest defensible closest parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one whole medium-sized hardtail mackerel (approximately 250 g), stuffed with sambal paste and deep-fried, served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — HPB Singapore / IMR Malaysia MyFCD: Deep-fried fish and sambal-based Malay dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient data for deep-fried mackerel and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for sambal-based preparations. The input record cited HPB Singapore as the primary source; values are retained and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entry for Atlantic mackerel, cooked (NDB 15046) and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional fish data. Composite values are consistent with the cited source range. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.2 g/100 g based on typical deep-fried mackerel fatty acid profiles from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for high-protein, high-fat deep-fried fish with spiced paste
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) deep-fried fish dishes with spiced paste having low carbohydrate content (5.2 g/100 g), (2) high protein (18.2 g) and fat (13.5 g) content substantially attenuating glycemic response, and (3) analogy with similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate fish preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 30–40). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 13.0) / 100 = 4.55, rounded to 4.6. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 5.2 × 2.5 = 13.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g) from belacan (shrimp paste) and added salt in sambal; deep-frying adds additional dietary fat load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving, request reduced belacan and salt in the sambal preparation, and pair with fresh ulam (raw vegetables) to offset sodium intake; daily sodium intake should remain below 2000 mg per Singapore HPB guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.2 g/100 g) and high protein (18.2 g/100 g) with a low estimated GI of 35, supporting stable postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal is a suitable main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load and high protein content; however, total meal glycemic load should be managed by limiting accompanying white rice to no more than half a cup (approximately 90 g cooked) per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat intake; however, hardtail mackerel provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish occasionally (once per week) and should prefer air-frying or grilling as a lower-fat alternative preparation; the omega-3 content of the mackerel is beneficial, but the deep-frying method and high sodium from belacan require moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mackerel provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; however, deep-frying and capsaicin-rich chilies in sambal may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Consume in moderation; consider grilled or baked preparation to reduce pro-inflammatory oxidized fats from deep-frying, and monitor individual tolerance to chili-based sambal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal, also known as Ikan Jaket Sumbat Sambal ('Jacket Fish' due to the hardtail mackerel's tough, leathery skin), is a classic Malay dish popular in Malaysia and Singapore. The dish is prepared by making deep slits along the backbone of a whole hardtail mackerel (Megalaspis cordyla) on both sides to create pockets, which are then generously stuffed with a thick, aromatic sambal paste made from dried chilies, shallots, garlic, ginger, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste). The stuffed fish is then deep-fried in hot oil until the skin is crispy and the sambal filling is fully cooked and fragrant. The result is a dish with a bold, spicy, and savory interior contrasting with the firm, meaty texture of the mackerel flesh. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 215 kcal, 18.2 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and only 5.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate main dish with a low estimated glycemic index of 35. The mackerel is a rich source of omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12 (5.5 mcg/100 g), niacin (7.5 mg/100 g), selenium (32 mcg/100 g), and vitamin D (4.5 mcg/100 g). Sodium content is relatively high at 420 mg/100 g due to belacan and added salt, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. It is traditionally served with steamed white rice and sometimes accompanied by Air Asam (tamarind dipping sauce) or kicap (soy sauce). This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a main protein source when rice portions are controlled, but should be consumed in moderation by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat content from deep-frying.

ingredient_synonyms.ikan cencaru: hardtail mackerel, Megalaspis cordyla, ikan jaket, jacket fish
ingredient_synonyms.belacan: shrimp paste, fermented prawn paste, blachan, blacan
ingredient_synonyms.sambal: chili paste, spiced chili condiment, sambal tumis

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from HPB Singapore and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for deep-fried mackerel and sambal-based Malay dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy mackerel data. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Saturated fat is estimated from typical deep-fried mackerel fatty acid profiles. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific Malay deep-fried stuffed fish preparation are not individually reported in HPB Singapore, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024; these fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data not available from any consulted approved source rather than confirmed zero. Saturated_fat_g (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy mackerel cooked data (NDB 15046) adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption. The input record incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan' as dietary tags; these have been removed as the dish contains fish and belacan (shrimp paste). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ikan Cencaru Sumbat Sambal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes are mapped to closest parent classes only; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this traditional Malay preparation. Multilingual names for Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this dish is consumed in Singapore's multicultural context; romanized Malay form is the primary local name.
